coredata.dc:title: Facile access to photo-switchable, dynamic-optical, multi-colored and solid-state materials from carbon dots and cellulose for photo-rewritable paper and advanced anti-counterfeiting
coredata.dc:creator: Jin, Kunfeng
Ji, Xin
Yang, Tiantian
Zhang, Jinming
Tian, Weiguo
Yu, Jian
Zhang, Xin
Chen, Zhangyan
Zhang, Jun
coredata.dc:description: Dynamic optical materials can respond to external stimuli to give intriguing and chameleon-like visual effect and carry more information, they hence have attracted considerable attention. In this work, we propose a simple and efficient strategy to construct multi-color solid-state carbon dots (CDs) materials with photo-switchable and reversible dynamic-optical properties. A photochromic and positively-charged cellulose derivative C-Im+-SP with an imidazole salt (Im+) group and a spiropyran (SP) group is designed and synthesized. Subsequently, C-Im+-SP is spontaneously coated on the surface of negatively-charged CDs (NCDs) via electrostatic attraction to form a physical protective shell, which not only inhibits aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) in NCDs but also promotes effective fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). The resultant NCDs@C-Im+-SP exhibits a photo-triggering reversible dynamic-optical property. The NCDs to C-Im+-SP ratio can be used to effectively regulate fluorescence emission and fluorescence kinetics. The polymeric C-Im+-SP shell layer endows NCDs with excellent processability and formability, whereby NCDs@C-Im+-SP materials can be utilized as functional coatings and inks to fabricate photo-rewritable paper and dynamic anti-counterfeit patterns that have considerable potential applications for special coatings, complex anti-counterfeiting, and information encryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dynamic optical materials
Solid fluorescence
Carbon dots
Cellulose
Photo-rewritable paper
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design of CDs-Based Dynamic Optical Materials
Preparation and Characterization of CDs-Based Dynamic Optical Materials
Photo-Rewritable Paper and Dynamic Fluorescent Patterns
Synthesis of Cellulose Derivative with Imidazole Salt Group (C-Im+)
Synthesis of Cellulose Derivative with Imidazole Salt Group and SP Group (C-Im+-SP)
Preparation of CDs@C-Im+-SP Powder and Film

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of design principle and mechanism of CDs-based dynamic optical materials.

Synthesis and characterization of C-Im+-SP and construction of NCDs@C-Im+-SP. (a) Synthetic route of C-Im+-SP. (b) 1H NMR spectra of C-Cl, C-Im+ and C-Im+-SP. (c) ζ-Potentials of NCDs, C-Im+-SP and NCDs@C-Im+-SP with various concentrations of C-Im+-SP (the concentration of NCDs is 5 mg/mL). (d) Dynamic light scattering (DLS) number mean size distribution curves of NCDs and NCDs@C-Im+-SP with various concentrations of C-Im+-SP (the concentration of NCDs is 5 mg/mL). (e) TEM image of NCDs@C-Im+-SP with 0.05% NCDs.

Dynamic fluorescence phenomenon and kinetics process of CDs@C-Im+-SP solid-state materials. (a) Overlap between the emission spectrum of NCDs and the absorption spectrum of C-Im+-SP. (b) Fluorescent images of CDs@C-Im+-SP with different C-Im+-SP concentration and different irradiation time (λex = 365 nm). (c-h) Fluorescence spectra and intensity at 460 and 658 nm of CDs@C-Im+-SP with NCDs concentration of (c, f) 0.1%, (d, g) 0.05% and (e, h) 0.01% as the increase of irradiation time (λex = 365 nm). (i) Fluorescence kinetic process of NCDs@C-Im+-SP.
(a) Photo-rewritable paper. (b) Multi-color dynamic fluorescence patterns.

head.ce:title: Facile access to photo-switchable, dynamic-optical, multi-colored and solid-state materials from carbon dots and cellulose for photo-rewritable paper and advanced anti-counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Engineering Plastics and CAS Research/Education Center for Excellence in Molecular Sciences, Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Engineering Plastics and CAS Research/Education Center for Excellence in Molecular Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CAS Key Laboratory of Engineering Plastics and CAS Research/Education Center for Excellence in Molecular Sciences, Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physical Science Laboratory, Huairou National Comprehensive Science Center, No. 5 Yanqi East Second Street, Beijing 101400, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Physical Science Laboratory, Huairou National Comprehensive Science Center, No. 5 Yanqi East Second Street, Beijing 101400, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Zhongguancun North First Street 2, 100190 Beijing, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dynamic optical materials can respond to external stimuli to give intriguing and chameleon-like visual effect and carry more information, they hence have attracted considerable attention. In this work, we propose a simple and efficient strategy to construct multi-color solid-state carbon dots (CDs) materials with photo-switchable and reversible dynamic-optical properties. A photochromic and positively-charged cellulose derivative C-Im+-SP with an imidazole salt (Im+) group and a spiropyran (SP) group is designed and synthesized. Subsequently, C-Im+-SP is spontaneously coated on the surface of negatively-charged CDs (NCDs) via electrostatic attraction to form a physical protective shell, which not only inhibits aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) in NCDs but also promotes effective fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). The resultant NCDs@C-Im+-SP exhibits a photo-triggering reversible dynamic-optical property. The NCDs to C-Im+-SP ratio can be used to effectively regulate fluorescence emission and fluorescence kinetics. The polymeric C-Im+-SP shell layer endows NCDs with excellent processability and formability, whereby NCDs@C-Im+-SP materials can be utilized as functional coatings and inks to fabricate photo-rewritable paper and dynamic anti-counterfeit patterns that have considerable potential applications for special coatings, complex anti-counterfeiting, and information encryption.

ce:section.ce:para: Dynamic optical materials exhibit remarkable changes in the emission wavelength, emission intensity and/or absorption wavelength under external stimuli, such as light, heat, electricity, force, and chemicals, thus they can be used to produce fascinating and chameleon-like visual effects and store more information. [1–11] Examples of commercial dynamic optical organic molecules include: azobenzene, spiropyran (SP), diarylethene, naphthopyran, fulgide, viologen, and polyaniline. [1–3] Examples of available dynamic optical inorganic materials include: metal oxides (WO3, NiO, Nb2O5, TiO2, MoO3, VO2, etc.), metal halides, rare-earth complexes, polyoxometalates, and alkaline earth sulfides. [12–15] Tian et al. synthesized a printable cellulose derivative containing SP groups (C-SP) that exhibited gradually increasing red fluorescence as the increase of UV irradiation time. A combination of C-SP and two other cellulose derivatives with blue and green fluorescence was used to produce easy-to-print chameleon-like patterns for application in advanced information encryption and complex anti-counterfeiting. [16] A series of polymer nanoparticles with photo-switchable full-colored emission was prepared by encapsulating fluorescent donors and photochromic acceptors in polymer micelles to create an efficient fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process. [17–19] The resultant nanoparticles could provide a noticeable improvement in fluorescent anti-counterfeiting and bioimaging. Kundu et al. used a switchable SP to modulate the assembly and dispersion of Au nanoparticles by light. [20] Various dynamic optical materials with stimuli-chromic behaviors were used as visual sensors to realize the real-time monitoring of a range of species, such as cations, anions, acids, solvents, biomolecules, temperature, pressure and even UV-light. [21–24] Electrochromic materials have been employed to fabricate smart windows, electronic displays, memory devices and electronic sensors. [25–28] The above mentioned reports demonstrate the availability, diversity and versatility of dynamic optical materials. Studies have also been performed on improving the properties and practical applications of dynamic optical materials in terms of the response selectivity, response sensitivity, adjustability of response rate, easy of preparation, biological safety, biocompatibility, photobleaching resistance, etc. Therefore, considerable attention is being paid to the development of novel high-performance dynamic optical materials, which are of great significance to further progress in advanced anti-counterfeiting, biological detection, visual recognition and other fields.

Carbon dots (CDs) are an emerging and promising class of fluorescent nanomaterials that contain a carbon core functionalized with various groups on the surface. CDs have a characteristic size of < 10 nm. CDs exhibit many unique advantages, such as low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, high photostability, adjustable luminescence, low cost, facile preparation, and abundant raw materials in nature; hence, CDs are being intensively investigated for optical applications (environmental monitoring, sensing, photodevices, and anti-counterfeiting), energy applications (solar photovoltaics, supercapacitors, and lithium-ion batteries), catalysis applications (organocatalysis, electrocatalysis, and photocatalysis) and biological applications (bioimaging, cancer therapy, gene regulation, and drug delivery). [29–38] Via controllable preparation processes and postsynthetic modification, continuous efforts have been devoted to design and construct CDs-based high-performance materials with novel properties, including phosphorescence [39–40], thermally activated delayed fluorescence [41], red emission [42–43], narrow bandwidth emission [44–45], upconversion emission [46–47], chirality [48–49], singlet oxygen (1O2) generation [50–51], and free-radical scavenging [52–53]. For instance, CDs-based long-lifetime room-temperature phosphorescence materials with different colors and special responsiveness can be obtained by heteroatom doping [54–55], immobilization in specific matrices (e.g. polyvinyl alcohol, polyurethane, and zeolites) [56–59], surface passivation [60–61], and crosslinking/aggregation networks [62–64]. Fan et al. demonstrated novel triangular CDs with multicolored narrow bandwidth emission (with a full width at half maximum of 30 nm) and a quantum yield up to 54–72%. [44] Yang et al. synthesized deep red emissive CDs with an unprecedentedly narrow full width at half maximum of 20 nm using natural taxus leaves as the raw materials. [45] Ge et al. prepared magnetofluorescent CDs-based materials, which not only effectively produced 1O2, but also worked as acidic H2O2-driven oxygenators. [65] However, owing to the intrinsic self-aggregation behavior of nanoscale-sized CDs, many CDs have severe aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) phenomenon, which substantially limits their application in solid-state fields. [66–72] In addition, CDs-based materials with dynamic optical properties are rarely reported. [73–76] Liao et al. demonstrated two strategies to construct CDs-based materials with reversible dynamic optical properties by chemical modification. A two-step chemical modification process was used to fix the SP molecule on the surface of CDs for obtaining photochromic CDs dispersion. [74] Besides, a macromolecular initiator was introduced onto the surface of the CDs, then initiated the polymerization reaction of styrene and SP with a vinyl group to fabricate photochromic films and nanofibers. [75–76]

In this work, taking advantage of CDs with strong fluorescence and charged surface and cellulose with environmental degradability and structural designability, we propose an efficient, universal and easy-to-operate method to fabricate novel CDs-based multi-color solid-state materials with photo-switchable and reversible dynamic-optical properties by constructing a physical protective shell and a FRET process. A cellulose derivative C-Im+-SP was synthesized with a positively-charged imidazole salt (Im+) group and a SP group, and subsequently immobilized on the surface of negatively-charged CDs (NCDs) via electrostatic attraction, thereby isolating the NCDs particles and inhibiting the ACQ effect. The efficient and adjustable FRET process between the NCDs and photochromic SP groups in C-Im+-SP produced CDs-based solid-state materials with photo-triggering reversible chromic behaviors under both UV light and visible light. Such CDs-based dynamic-optical materials serve as functional coatings and inks to prepare photo-rewritable paper and dynamic anti-counterfeit patterns.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of CDs-Based Dynamic Optical Materials
ce:section.ce:para: To construct dynamic optical materials with multi-color changes, FRET is an effective strategy. [16–19] The FRET process has three essential components: a luminescent donor, an energy acceptor, and a small distance between the donor and the acceptor. The emission spectrum of the luminescent donor should have a considerable overlap with the absorption spectrum of the energy acceptor. In addition, the emission spectrum of the acceptor should be clearly different from that of the donor. Meanwhile, the emission intensity of the acceptor or/and the donor must change dramatically as the increase of irradiation time. More importantly, the donor and the acceptor must be in close range, corresponding to a separation of approximately 1 to 10 nm to ensure an effective FRET process. Based on the above-mentioned requirements for fabricating dynamic optical materials via an efficient FRET process, CDs with strong fluorescence and charged surfaces can be employed as a model FRET donor, and a charged polymer with a wide-band absorbing and photochromic SP group can serve as the FRET acceptor. Electrostatic attraction drives the charged polymer to spontaneously coat the surface of CDs, forming a core–shell structure that effectively decreases the distance between the donor and the acceptor to satisfy the distance requirements of the FRET process (Fig. 1 ). CDs absorb 365 nm UV light to reach the excited singlet state. Then, some of the excited CDs return to the ground state accompanied by a blue fluorescence emission, and other excited CDs transfer energy to the SP groups in the charged polymer by a resonance mode, which promotes the red fluorescence emission of the SP groups. As a finite time is required for the SP groups to convert from a ring-closed form to a ring-open form (merocyanine, MC), the energy transfer process and efficiency between CDs and SP groups change with time. As a result, with increasing the UV irradiation time, the red fluorescence gradually strengthens, and the blue fluorescence gradually weakens, finally an equilibrium fluorescence state is reached. At the same time, the visible color of the CDs@charged polymer becomes red. Photo-triggered chromic behaviors under both UV light and visible light are realized. The final fluorescence emission depends on the ratio of the CDs to the SP group, which can be used to finely regulate the energy transfer efficiency between the FRET donor and acceptor. Subsequently, switching to strong visible light irradiation transforms the SP groups from the ring-open form to the ring-closed form, resulting in the disappearance of the red fluorescence and the visible red color. Thus, CDs-based multi-color materials with photo-switchable and reversible dynamic-optical properties can be fabricated by constructing a physical protective shell and a FRET process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and Characterization of CDs-Based Dynamic Optical Materials
ce:section.ce:para: For achieving the above design, biopolymer cellulose with environmental degradability and structural designability was chosen as the polymer matrix. Taking advantage of the homogeneous and controllable modification process, a positively-charged cellulose derivative, C-Im+-SP, was synthesized by introducing an imidazole salt group (Im+) and a SP group onto the cellulose chain. The synthesis route is shown in Fig. 2 a. First, cellulose 2-chloropropionate (C-Cl) with a degree of substitution (DS) of 2.1 was prepared by a homogeneous esterification reaction in an ionic liquid 1-allyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride (AmimCl). In the 1H NMR spectrum of C-Cl (Fig. 2b), the peak at 1.59 ppm was attributed to the methyl protons of 2-chloropropionate, and the peaks at 2.70–6.00 ppm were assigned to the cellulose skeleton protons and methenyl protons of 2-chloropropionate. Then, after an addition reaction between C-Cl and 1-butylimidazole, a cellulose derivative C-Im+ containing the Im+ group was obtained. In the 1H NMR spectrum of C-Im+ (Fig. 2b), the new peaks at 0.87, 1.24, 1.79 and 4.24 ppm were assigned to the butyl protons of Im+ group, and the two new peaks at 7.88 and 9.79 ppm were assigned to the imidazole protons of the Im+ group. According to the integral area ratio of the cellulose skeleton and the imidazole protons, the DS of Im+ was calculated to be 2.1. Finally, C-Im+-SP was obtained by covalent immobilization of the SP group onto C-Im+ by using 4,4′-methylene diphenyl diisocyanate (MDI) as a bridge linker. In the 1H NMR spectrum of C-Im+-SP, the new peaks at 7.33 and 7.07 ppm were attributed to the aromatic protons of MDI, indicating the successful preparation of C-Im+-SP. However, the SP content in C-Im+-SP was too low to be accurately calculated by 1H NMR method. The DS of SP was 0.0082, which was determined by the UV absorption method (Fig. S1) instead of the 1H NMR method. The resultant C-Im+-SP clearly exhibits a photoinduced fluorochromic phenomenon (Fig. S2). The red fluorescence emission at 651 nm of C-Im+-SP gradually strengthens as the irradiation time increases, and the fluorescence intensity reaches a maximum after 240 s of irradiation.

One kind of negatively-charged CDs, NCDs, was obtained by a hydrothermal process from citric acid and ethylenediamine (Fig. S3). TEM image shows that the particle diameter of NCDs is 3.55 nm (Fig. S4). NCDs emit strong blue fluorescence (λem = 452 nm) in dilute solution, while the blue fluorescence of NCDs almost completely quenches in solid state, suggesting that NCDs have a typical ACQ behavior (Figs. S5 and S6). The dilute NCDs solution was coated onto the surface of filter papers to get blue-fluorescence papers. The fluorescence paper remains at the initial fluorescence intensity after UV irradiation for a long time (Fig. S7), indicating that NCDs have no dynamic optical properties.

Once the negatively-charged NCDs solution was mixed with the positively-charged C-Im+-SP solution, C-Im+-SP was spontaneously absorbed on the surface of each nanoparticle of NCDs to forms a shell layer, owing to the strong electrostatic attractions. As the C-Im+-SP concentration increases, the ζ-potential of NCDs@C-Im+-SP solutions gradually increases from negative to positive (Fig. 2c). Meanwhile, the particle size in NCDs@C-Im+-SP solutions increases markedly (Fig. 2d). TEM image directly confirms the formation of a thick C-Im+-SP shell layer on the surface of the NCDs (Fig. 2e). These phenomena confirm that the positively-charged C-Im+-SP surrounds the negatively-charged NCDs, which effectively isolates the nanoparticles of NCDs from each other. Thus, the ACQ phenomenon of NCDs is inhibited, and NCDs@C-Im+-SP solid-state materials with bright fluorescence and photoinduced chromic behavior are obtained (Fig. S8). The NCDs@C-Im+-SP suspension was obtained by precipitating NCDs@C-Im+-SP/DMF in acetone. The NCDs@C-Im+-SP aqueous solution was freeze-dried to produce a NCDs@C-Im+-SP solid powder. Via a doctor-blade casting process and wet-spinning process, NCDs@C-Im+-SP film and fiber with dynamic optical properties were prepared. The resultant suspension and film initially emit bright blue fluorescence under UV irradiation, and gradually turn red as the irradiation time increases. The visible color changes to red. In addition, NCDs@C-Im+-SP solution can be also utilized to fabricate fluorescent coatings with photo-triggered chromic behaviors. Moreover, the C-Im+-SP polymer chains on the surface of NCDs nanoparticles make NCDs@C-Im+-SP coatings superior to NCDs coatings in terms of formability and stability (Fig. S9). Thus, the formation of C-Im+-SP shell not only effectively separates NCDs nanoparticles, which inhibits the ACQ phenomenon of NCDs, but also endows NCDs with dynamic optical properties, excellent processability, good formability and superior stability.

Electrostatic attraction causes C-Im+-SP to spontaneously form a tight coating layer on the surface of NCDs, ensuring a sufficiently close distance between SP and NCDs. Additionally, most of the emission spectrum of NCDs, the FRET donor, overlaps with the absorption spectrum of C-Im+-SP, the FRET acceptor (Fig. 3 a). Thus, an effective FRET process can be established between C-Im+-SP and NCDs (Fig. S10). Under UV irradiation, NCDs are excited to the excited state. Some of the excited NCDs return to the ground state accompanied by a blue fluorescence emission, whereas other excited NCDs transfer energy to SP groups by a resonance mode, promoting a red fluorescence emission. The obtained NCDs@C-Im+-SP shows dynamic optical behavior: as the UV irradiation time increases, the red fluorescence gradually strengthens, and the blue fluorescence gradually weakens until an equilibrium fluorescence state is reached (Fig. 3b). The C-Im+-SP content in the NCDs@C-Im+-SP can be adjusted to regulate the final fluorescence color of the NCDs@C-Im+-SP to purple, pink or red.

The fluorescence spectra of NCDs@C-Im+-SP materials under different irradiation time visually illustrate their fluorescence kinetic process (Fig. 3c-3 h). As the UV irradiation time increases, the intensity of blue fluorescence at 460 nm gradually decreases, and the red fluorescence at 658 nm gradually increases, indicating a typical photoinduced fluorochromic phenomenon. By changing the content of NCDs in NCDs@C-Im+-SP (0.1%, 0.05%, and 0.01%), the ratio of blue/red fluorescence and the final fluorescence color can be controlled. Furthermore, the NCDs content in NCDs@C-Im+-SP has a significant impact on the chromic rate. As shown in Fig. 3f-3 h, the higher the NCDs content, the shorter the time to reach equilibrium is, which is attributed to the increase in the energy supplied by the FRET donor. These phenomena indicate that NCDs and C-Im+-SP form a typical and effective FRET process.

The fluorescence kinetic process of NCDs@C-Im+-SP consists of four main steps: NCDs excitation (k1) and emission (kf), C-Im+-SP isomerization (k3 and k5), C-Im+-MC excitation (k4) and emission (kf), and FRET process (Fig. 3i). According to the Franck-Condon rule, the energy absorption, FRET and fluorescence emission processes are much faster than C-Im+-SP isomerization process, which is therefore the rate-determining step in the NCDs@C-Im+-SP fluorescence dynamic process and strongly depends on the donor/acceptor ratio in NCDs@C-Im+-SP. Kinetic fitting plots of the change in the fluorescence intensity at 460 and 658 nm have been made to get the fluorescence kinetic order of NCDs@C-Im+-SP (Fig. S11 and S12 and Table S1). The results suggest that the kinetic order of the fluorescence variation at 460 nm strongly depends on the NCDs content. For 0.01% NCDs in NCDs@C-Im+-SP, the change in the fluorescence intensity at 460 nm is a first-order kinetic process. Above 0.05% NCDs, the change in the fluorescence intensity at 460 nm is a non-first-order kinetic process. The kinetic order of the fluorescence intensity at 658 nm is independent of the NCDs content in NCDs@C-Im+-SP. The change in the fluorescence intensity at 658 nm is a first-order kinetic process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photo-Rewritable Paper and Dynamic Fluorescent Patterns
ce:section.ce:para: The cellulose derivative C-Im+-SP shell not only endows NCDs with dynamic optical properties, but also provides excellent processability and formability for NCDs@C-Im+-SP materials. NCDs@C-Im+-SP film and fiber can be readily fabricated (Fig. S8). More impressively, NCDs@C-Im+-SP can act as coatings or inks for the facile preparation of photo-rewritable paper and dynamic anti-counterfeiting patterns easily. Filter paper is immersed in NCDs@C-Im+-SP solution and dried to obtain photo-rewritable paper (Fig. 4 a and S13). Various types of information can be written on the paper using UV light instead of traditional inks. The writing process can be monitored by a visual real-time mode based on the change of the fluorescence color from blue to red. The UV irradiation time controls the darkness of the visible color, which can provide additional functions, such as information encryption and anti-counterfeiting. The information or patterns are readily erased under strong visible light irradiation. A photo-triggered “write-erase” process is realized by alternating between UV light and visible light. The “write-erase” process can be repeated several times, indicating that the photo-rewritable paper can be reused many times.

C-Im+-SP, NCDs@C-Im+-SP with different C-Im+-SP content and two other CDs@cellulose derivatives with green and orange fluorescence [77] were used as inks to print different fluorescence patterns. C-Im+-SP and NCDs@C-Im+-SP have dynamic optical properties. Moreover, the final fluorescence and fluorescence kinetics of NCDs@C-Im+-SP can be adjusted by controlling the C-Im+-SP content. Hence, complex dynamic full-color anti-counterfeiting patterns were fabricated by regulating the composition of inks, as shown in Fig. 4b. The patterns can be designed and printed according to specific requirements. Thus, “simple preparation, complex anti-counterfeiting” is realized.

Note that both the CDs and cellulose derivatives used in the preparation of NCDs@C-Im+-SP dynamic optical materials are environmentally friendly and easy to process. The resultant NCDs@C-Im+-SP exhibits noticeable photo-triggered chromic behavior, adjustable fluorescence color and fluorescence kinetics, and excellent processability and formability. Therefore, NCDs@C-Im+-SP has excellent application prospects for special coatings, complex anti-counterfeiting patterns and information encryption.

Reversible-chromic multi-color solid-state materials from CDs were fabricated using a simple strategy based on the spontaneous formation of a protective shell of cellulose derivatives and a FRET process. A photochromic and positively-charged cellulose derivative, C-Im+-SP, was synthesized and spontaneously coated onto the surface of negatively-charged NCDs via electrostatic attraction to form a physical protective shell. The C-Im+-SP shell not only inhibits ACQ phenomenon of NCDs but also promotes an effective FRET process between NCDs and SP. The resultant NCDs@C-Im+-SP exhibits reversible dynamic-optical properties. Adjusting the NCDs to C-Im+-SP ratio can effectively regulate the fluorescence emission and fluorescence kinetics. Such a strategy is facile, effective and universal to fabricate CDs-based dynamic-optical solid-state materials. Furthermore, the polymeric C-Im+-SP shell layer endows NCDs with excellent processability and formability, so the NCDs@C-Im+-SP materials have been utilized as functional coatings and inks to fabricate photo-rewritable paper and dynamic anti-counterfeit patterns, which have excellent potential application in special coatings, complex anti-counterfeiting, and information encryption.

All organic solvents, including N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), acetone, ethanol and ethylenediamine (EDA), were analytical grade and obtained from Beijing Beihua Fine Chemicals Co., Ltd. Extra dry DMF with molecular sieves (99.9%, water ≤ 50 ppm) was purchased from Beijing InnoChem Science & Technology Co., Ltd. 2-Chloropropionyl chloride, 1-butylimidazole (BI, 99%) and 4,4′-methylene diphenyl diisocyanate (MDI, 98%) were purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. 1-(2-Hydroxyethyl)-3,3-dimethylindolino-6′-nitrobenzopyrylospiran (SPOH, 93%) was purchased from TCI Development Co., Ltd. Citric acid monohydrate was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Microcrystalline cellulose (MCC) with a degree of polymerization of 220 and AmimCl were provided by Shandong ICCAS-Henglian Biobased Materials Co., Ltd. All reagents were used directly as received.

The NCDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method as follows. A total of 10 mL of deionized water was mixed with 0.42 g (2 mmol) of citric acid monohydrate and 536 μL (8 mmol) of EDA. The resultant solution was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (25 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. The reactors were cooled to room temperature naturally. The obtained suspension solution was purified through dialysis in a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of 3500 Da. Finally, the purified solution was freeze-dried to obtain NCDs powder.

A total of 0.972 g (6 mmol) of MCC was added into 20 g of AmimCl. The mixture was stirred vigorously at 80 °C for 2 h to obtain a transparent MCC/AmimCl solution. To decrease the viscosity, 5 mL of extra dry DMF was added. Subsequently, 2.2855 g (18 mmol) of 2-chloropropionyl chloride was added into the solution under stirring at 0 °C. The solution was heated to 40 °C and stirred vigorously for 2 h. The reaction was terminated by the addition of an ethanol/water mixture (v/v, 1:1). The product was filtered, washed three times with ethanol, and dried in vacuum oven at 80 °C. According to the integral area ratio of the cellulose skeleton and the methyl group in 1H NMR spectrum of C-Cl, the degree of substitution (DS) is calculated to be 2.1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Cellulose Derivative with Imidazole Salt Group (C-Im+)
ce:section.ce:para: A total of 0.7041 g (2 mmol) of C-Cl was added into 15 mL of DMF. The mixture was stirred vigorously at 80 °C until the C-Cl dissolved completely. Then, 0.7823 g (6.3 mmol) of BI were added into the solution and allowed to react at 80 °C for 24 h. After the reaction, the final product was precipitated in acetone, washed three times, and dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C. According to the integral area ratio of the cellulose skeleton and the imidazole protons, the DS of Im+ is calculated to be 2.1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Cellulose Derivative with Imidazole Salt Group and SP Group (C-Im+-SP)
ce:section.ce:para: A total of 0.0501 g (0.2 mmol) of MDI was added into 5 mL of extra dry DMF and dissolved under light stirring at 60 °C in a N2 atmosphere. A total of 0.0705 g (0.21 mmol) of SPOH was added and dissolved in 5 mL of extra dry DMF. Subsequently, the SPOH/DMF solution was added dropwise in the MDI/DMF solution in a N2 atmosphere. The solution was stirred vigorously at 60 °C for 6 h to obtain a MDI-SPOH/DMF solution. A total of 0.1905 g (0.4 mmol) of C-Im+ was added and dissolved in 5 mL of extra dry DMF. The MDI-SPOH/DMF solution was added to the C-Im+/DMF solution in a N2 atmosphere and allowed to react at 80 °C for 24 h. After the reaction, the final product was precipitated in acetone, washed three times, and dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs@C-Im+-SP Powder and Film
ce:section.ce:para: Two methods have been used to fabricate NCDs@C-Im+-SP powders. In the first method, NCDs/methanol solution (1 mg/mL) was added to a C-Im+-SP/DMF solution (20 mg/mL) under stirring for 2 h. Then, the as-prepared C-Im+-SP/DMF solution was added into acetone to get C-Im+-SP suspension. After centrifugation and drying, NCDs@C-Im+-SP powder was obtained. In the second method, NCDs aqueous solutions with various concentrations of 0.01–10 mg/mL were added to a C-Im+-SP aqueous solution (20 mg/mL) under stirring for 6 h. The obtained solutions were freeze-dried to obtain NCDs@C-Im+-SP powders.

The NCDs/methanol solution (1 mg/mL) was added to the C-Im+-SP/DMF solution (20 mg/mL) under stirring for 2 h. The obtained CDs@C-Im+-SP/DMF solution was concentrated by a solvent evaporation process, then cast onto a glass plate. After drying at 50 °C, the NCDs@C-Im+-SP film was obtained.

The NCDs/methanol solution (1 mg/mL) was added to the C-Im+-SP/DMF solution (20 mg/mL) under stirring for 2 h to obtain a CDs@C-Im+-SP/DMF solution. Then, the DMF-treated filter paper was immersed into the CDs@C-Im+-SP/DMF solution. Finally, the filter paper was dried at 80 °C to produce the photo-rewritable paper.

The NCDs@C-Im+-SP/DMF, C-Im+-SP/DMF and CDs@cellulose derivative/H2O [77] solutions were used as anti-counterfeiting inks. Airplane and hot air balloon patterns were printed on nonfluorescent papers, then the above anti-counterfeiting inks were brushed onto the different parts of these patterns. After drying at 80 °C, the multi-color anti-counterfeiting patterns were obtained.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was conducted using a Hitachi HT-7700 with an accelerating voltage of 120 kV. 1H NMR spectra were obtained on a Bruker AV-400 NMR spectrometer with DMSO‑d6 as the internal standard. Zeta potential and size distribution were recorded on a Malvern Zetasize Nano-ZS90. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained using a PerkinElmer Lambda 35 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were recorded with a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Beijing Municipal Science & Technology Commission (Z191100007219009), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51773210 and 51803220), and the Youth Innovation Promotion Association CAS (2018040).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: DS detection of SP; UV-Vis absorption spectra; Fluorescence spectra; Synthetic route of NCDs; TEM images; Photographs of CDs-based powder, film and fibers; Stability of CDs-based coatings; Fluorescence kinetics; Photo-rewritable paper and UV writing patterns. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2020.126794.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
